did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) approach, specifically fine-tuning an EfficientNet model, for the detection and classification of malignant melanoma in skin lesions. The study employed AI clustering techniques (PCA, k-means, t-SNE) on a combined dataset from the 2019 and 2020 IIM-ISIC Melanoma Classification Challenges to improve classification performance, achieving a ROC-AUC of 99.48%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper describes original experimental work involving the development, fine-tuning, and evaluation of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model for detecting malignant melanoma. It details specific methodology including dataset usage (ISIC 2019/2020), model architecture (EfficientNet-B6), clustering techniques, and reports new quantitative results (ROC-AUC scores) from cross-fold validation experiments.

title: Intraclass Clustering-Based CNN Approach for Detection of Malignant Melanoma
brief_summary: This study developed a classification model for the effective detection of malignant melanoma in skin lesions by fine-tuning a state-of-the-art Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). The researchers utilized a combined dataset from the 2019 and 2020 IIM-ISIC Melanoma Classification Challenges to train and evaluate their models. The methodology involved applying various AI clustering techniques, including PCA and k-means, alongside dimensionality reduction methods like t-SNE to improve feature representation. The models were assessed using varying cross-fold validations to determine their diagnostic performance. The primary result was a significant improvement in classification metrics, with the best model achieving a ROC-AUC score of 99.48%. The findings suggest that AI-assisted clustering can enhance the detection of malignant melanoma compared to previous challenge-winning solutions.

all_task_types.target: Malignant Melanoma detection and multi-class skin lesion classification (including Melanocytic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Actinic Keratosis, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Vascular Lesion, Dermatofibroma, and Not Classified).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the development of a 'classification model' for the 'detection of malignant melanoma'. The study investigates binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) and multi-class classification (up to 7 classes) using an EfficientNet-B6 CNN. The primary target is the identification of the malignant melanoma class. Performance was evaluated using ROC-AUC, AUC, precision, recall, and F1-score, which are standard metrics for classification tasks. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion classes) rather than continuous numeric outcomes.

datasets_used.name: Combined ISIC 2019 and 2020 Melanoma Classification Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A combined dataset of dermoscopic images from the 2019 and 2020 IIM-ISIC Melanoma Classification Challenges. After removing duplicate records, it contains images from approximately 2000 patients of all age groups. The dataset includes tfrecord values allowing for stratified k-fold validation.

use.training: Used to train and fine-tune the EfficientNet-B6 base model. The dataset was used in varying cross-fold validations (up to 15-fold) to evaluate model performance.
use.validation: Used for cross-fold validation to assess model stability and performance metrics (ROC-AUC, precision, recall, F1-score).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the best-performing models via cross-fold validation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate model performance and stability across different subsets of the dataset.
To ensure balanced distribution of the melanoma proportion across validation folds.
To reduce dimensionality of features and capture variance for clustering and model input.
To categorize data points into clusters based on Euclidean distance to improve classification performance.
To group data points based on minimum or maximum distance metrics for comparative analysis.
To visualize multi-dimensional space in two dimensions and highlight clusters within the melanoma class.
To perform linear transformation on data for clustering based on standard deviation and nearest neighbours.
To investigate non-linear boundaries between classifiers as an alternative to the final layer.
To investigate linear boundaries between classifiers as an alternative to the final layer.
To verify the optimal formation of clusters in K-means by describing how well each label fits its designated cluster.
To determine the suitable number of clusters in K-means by plotting against the number of clusters.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using ROC-AUC, AUC, precision, recall, and F1-score via cross-fold validation.

Combined ISIC 2019 and 2020 Melanoma Cla.evaluation_setting: Cross-fold validation (up to 15-fold) on a subset of 5024 images; final evaluation on 8 transposes/flips

Combined ISIC 2019 and 2020 Melanoma Cla.notes: Model T_25 achieved the highest AUC score of 0.9962. Model E_7 was identified as the most effective classification model based on ROC-AUC, precision, and F1-score. The study reports a baseline increase of 0.05% in ROC-AUC compared to the winning solution of the 2020 IIM-ISIC Melanoma Classification Challenge [15]. The highest ROC-AUC reached 99.48%.

architecture_details.description: The base model uses the EfficientNet-B6 architecture, originally pretrained on ImageNet. The architecture comprises 667 layers built from specific modules. The penultimate layer outputs 2304 features.

training_details.training_method: Fine-tuning and retraining on a combined dataset of ISIC 2019 and 2020 images. The last layer was retrained using various clustering techniques (PCA, t-SNE, k-means, NCA) applied to the penultimate layer features.

classification_or_output_head.details: The final layer structure was changed/retrained. Alternative classifiers tested included Quadratic Discriminant Analysis (QDA) and Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) as replacements for the final layer. The standard approach involved retraining the last layer based on clustered labels derived from the 2304-dimensional feature vector of the penultimate layer.

preprocessing_techniques: Unity-based normalization (rescaling) of features before clustering
Removal of duplicate records from the combined dataset

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Each test image was evaluated in eight different transposes/flips, and the average result was obtained to calculate the final ROC-AUC score.

conclusions: The study developed a classification model for the effective detection of malignant melanoma by fine-tuning a state-of-the-art Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), specifically EfficientNet-B6.

The proposed approach achieved a highest ROC-AUC score of 99.48% (0.9948) and an AUC score of 0.9962, representing a small but measurable improvement over the winning solution of the 2020 IIM-ISIC Melanoma Classification Challenge.
The most effective model (E_7) was identified as the one retrained on 5 classes using PCA and k-means clustering, demonstrating that AI clustering techniques can enhance classification performance.

The study investigated various AI clustering techniques, including PCA, t-SNE, k-means, and Neighbourhood Component Analysis (NCA), applied to the penultimate layer features of the CNN to improve feature representation and clustering results.

Alternative classifiers Quadratic Discriminant Analysis (QDA) and Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) were tested as replacements for the final layer but did not achieve performance levels comparable to the CNN-based approaches with clustering.
The methodology allows for automatic feature selection through dimensionality reduction and clustering, offering an advantage over methods requiring manual feature selection by experts.

The study utilized a combined dataset from the 2019 and 2020 IIM-ISIC Melanoma Classification Challenges, evaluating performance using varying cross-fold validations to ensure stability across different classification thresholds.
important_remarks: The study relies on a combined dataset from the 2019 and 2020 IIM-ISIC Melanoma Classification Challenges, which may introduce dataset bias and limits generalizability to external clinical populations.

The paper explicitly states that the use of CNNs for medical diagnosis is generally not permitted in health institutions due to GDPR regulations regarding the 'right to explanation', highlighting significant deployment and regulatory barriers.
The model is designed as an alerting tool to assist dermatologists rather than a substitute for professional diagnosis, acknowledging the need for further analytical procedures.

The reported performance improvements over the 2020 challenge winner are described as 'small' (e.g., 0.05% increase in ROC-AUC), suggesting the clinical significance of the marginal gains may be limited.
The study acknowledges that limited data availability in existing datasets often causes racial bias with respect to certain skin types, a limitation inherent to the source data.

No external funding was received, and the Institutional Review Board and Informed Consent statements are marked as 'Not applicable', indicating reliance on publicly available challenge data without new ethical oversight for this specific analysis.
